_notes: Colours are fixed to MODAL FUNCTION, not individual sentences.
Every modal word maps to a consistent hex/bg pair via the palette below.
The 'palette' object is the single source of truth — the HTML tool reads it.
Families: certainty | possibility | obligation | permission | advice | volition | habit | functional
request | offer | suggestion | refusal | invitation | permission_ask
context_error: true means the modal is grammatically valid but wrong for this register/situation.
ok: false + no context_error = grammar/logic error (uses ERROR palette key).
ok: false + context_error: true = register error (keeps real palette colour, different UI treatment possible).
question: true on a modal = append ? to the rendered sentence. Omit for statement-form suggestions.

sentences.context: A manager speaking encouragingly to an employee.
sentences.before: If you finish the report, you

modals.note: The most neutral choice — a straightforward promise about the result.
Slightly more tentative than 'will'. The outcome is likely but not guaranteed.
Much weaker. The speaker isn't very sure — feelings are unpredictable.
Sounds almost like a logical conclusion: given how hard you worked, pride is the only possible response.

modals.gloss: Not used in modern neutral English
modals.note: 'Shall' is archaic or legalistic here. You'd only meet it in very formal or old-fashioned writing.

sentences.context: Two friends planning a picnic, looking at the sky.
sentences.before: If the weather stays fine, we

modals.note: A confident intention — the picnic is on.
Frames the fine weather as unlocking an option that isn't currently available.
A gentle proposal — more exploratory than 'can', slightly hypothetical in feel.
The speaker hasn't committed yet — eating outside is one of several options.
Frames eating outside as the right thing to do — almost an obligation to enjoy the weather.

sentences.context: Looking at a dark house across the street. No lights, no car in the drive.

modals.note: The visual evidence is strong. You are highly confident — perhaps 95% sure.
About 50-50 — they might be away, or they might just be out for the evening.
Similar to 'might' but slightly more neutral — flagging something as technically possible without committing.
Very close in meaning to 'might', just a touch more formal in tone.
'Can' works for general theoretical possibility ('accidents can happen') but is not used for guessing about a specific, current situation.

sentences.context: A colleague didn't come to the meeting. You have no idea why — she gave no warning.

modals.note: You are almost entirely sure this is the reason. The evidence points strongly here.
A viable possibility, but you acknowledge there are other potential explanations too.
Very close to 'might have'. Both function well here.
The formal equivalent of 'might have' — commonly found in professional writing and news reports.
Grammatically correct, but logically wrong. 'Should have forgotten' implies she was supposed to forget — which makes no sense here.

sentences.context: A friend is struggling to sleep and asks what to do.

modals.note: The everyday, natural choice for offering helpful advice. Warm and direct.
Slightly old-fashioned and more formal, but still grammatically correct.
Much stronger — implies guaranteed negative consequences if the advice is ignored.
Very forceful — closer to a strict rule than casual advice. Use when the recommendation is urgent.
'Don't have to' signals no obligation — the opposite of advice against something. A very common learner error.

sentences.context: A student missed an important deadline.

modals.note: The standard choice to point out what someone failed to do but ought to have done.
Softer than 'should have'. It highlights the overlooked possibility rather than the failure itself.
The gentlest option — phrasing the criticism almost as a subtle hint rather than a direct complaint.
Virtually identical in meaning to 'should have', simply more elevated in register.
'Must have told me' shifts the function entirely — it becomes a deduction ('I'm certain you told me'), stripping away the criticism.

sentences.context: In an open-plan office. You want a colleague to lower their voice on a call.
sentences.after: speak a little more quietly, please?

modals.note: Perfectly acceptable between colleagues. Some speakers consider it slightly blunt, but it is natural and very common.
The safe all-purpose choice. Slightly more tentative than 'can' — it softens the request without sounding stiff.
More formal than 'could'. Signals real respect for the other person's time or effort.
Common in informal speech, but can sound demanding in writing or to someone you don't know well.
'Shall you' is not used in English. 'Shall' in questions works only with I or we: 'Shall I open a window?'

sentences.context: Emailing a senior colleague you haven't met. You need them to review a document.
sentences.after: be able to cast an eye over the attached document.

modals.word: I was wondering if you might
modals.note: The double distancing ('was wondering' + 'might') makes this one of the most considerate request structures in English. Ideal for formal written requests.
Clear and professional. Very suitable for written communication with someone senior.
Appropriate here, though marginally less deferential than 'Would you' for a first formal contact.
Grammatically fine, but register mismatch — 'Can you' reads as casual in a formal email to someone you don't know.
'Will you' in a formal email can come across as presumptuous — it implies the other person has already agreed. Avoid in written requests to seniors.

sentences.context: A guest arrives at your home on a cold evening.
sentences.after: I make you a cup of tea?

modals.note: The classic British structure for first-person offers. Warm, spontaneous, and very natural.
Friendly and common. Slightly less idiomatic than 'shall' for an offer, but perfectly natural.
Slightly softer — leaves the guest more room to decline without awkwardness.
'Would I make you tea?' sounds like a genuine question about your own behaviour, not an offer. 'Would you like me to make tea?' is the correct pattern.
'Must I make you tea?' implies reluctant duty. It would sound very strange and slightly hostile as an offer.

sentences.context: You and a friend are trying to decide where to have lunch.
sentences.after: try that new Italian place on the corner.

modals.note: Invites agreement from the group. Very natural British English for joint decisions.
Extremely common in spoken English. The question form softens what is actually a proposal.
Frames the idea as one possibility among others — gives everyone room to suggest alternatives.
Slightly stronger than 'could' — the speaker genuinely thinks this is the best option.
Only works if you're genuinely excited about the idea. Otherwise it sounds pushy.

sentences.context: Someone asks you to cover their shift at work at very short notice.

modals.note: The most socially safe refusal. Blurs the line between 'unable' and 'unwilling' — which is often intentional.
Honest, but blunt. Signals a decision, not an inability. Use carefully — it can sound confrontational.
Slightly more hesitant than 'can't' — leaves a small opening, as if circumstances might change.
The conditional phrasing adds distance — it sounds considered and formal rather than reactive.
'I mustn't do it' means 'I'm not allowed to' — implying a rule is stopping you, not your own choice or schedule. Sounds very unusual here.

sentences.context: In a library. You want to use your laptop but aren't sure if it's allowed.

modals.note: Very common in everyday speech. Prescriptive grammarians once objected, but 'Can I?' is now fully standard.
Slightly more tentative and respectful than 'Can I'. A safe choice in any context.
The most traditionally correct form for asking permission. More formal than 'can' or 'could' — very polite.
'Might I?' is very formal and somewhat old-fashioned. Still used, but can sound overly deferential in modern speech.
'Shall I?' offers to do something for someone else, it doesn't ask permission for yourself. Wrong function here.

sentences.context: You knock on your neighbour's door. Music is playing inside and you can see a light on.

modals.note: The music and the light are evidence. 'Must' signals that you are reasoning from what you can see — it's not a guess, it's a conclusion.
If you said 'Someone can't be in' you would be reasoning to the opposite conclusion — perhaps because the car is gone and it's very quiet.
'Might' doesn't use the evidence — it just flags a possibility. Weaker and less precise than 'must' when you have clear evidence.
Even more tentative than 'might'. Fine if you're uncertain, but misses the strength of the evidence in front of you.
'Should be in' means you expect them to be in — perhaps because they promised to stay home. It's about expectation, not deduction from evidence.

sentences.context: Your friend ordered a pizza forty minutes ago. The delivery app says it left the restaurant thirty-five minutes ago.

modals.note: The timing is the evidence. 'Should' here means 'I expect it to, based on what I know' — a softer deduction than 'must'.
Stronger than 'should' — you're very confident based on the evidence. Both work, but 'must' leaves less room for doubt.
'Might' is fine but doesn't use the timing information. It treats arrival as a mere possibility rather than a near-certain conclusion.
'It can't arrive soon' would mean the evidence points the other way — perhaps it only left two minutes ago. Here the evidence says the opposite.
'Mustn't arrive' would mean you're forbidding the pizza from coming — which makes no sense. 'Mustn't' is never used for deduction.

sentences.context: You get home and find the front door unlocked. Your flatmate's coat and keys are on the hook.
sentences.after: come home while I was out.

modals.note: The coat and keys are physical evidence. 'Must have' signals a confident past conclusion — you're not guessing, you're reasoning.

modals.gloss: Past possibility — weaker than the evidence warrants
modals.note: Grammatically fine, but with this much evidence 'might have' undersells the conclusion. Use it when evidence is thin or ambiguous.
The weakest of the three. Appropriate when you're genuinely unsure, less so when the evidence is as clear as this.
'Can't have come home' would mean the evidence points the other way. Here it directly contradicts what you can see.
'Should have come home' is a criticism — implying they were supposed to but perhaps didn't. It has nothing to do with deduction from evidence.

sentences.context: A friend hasn't replied to your message for two days. You have no idea why.

modals.note: You're not reasoning from clues — you're just floating a possibility. 'Might' is the natural choice when you genuinely don't know.
Very close to 'might' here. 'Could' sometimes implies one of several possibilities — 'she could be busy, or she could have lost her phone.'
Essentially the same meaning as 'might' but a touch more formal in tone.

modals.gloss: Too strong — implies evidence you don't have
modals.note: 'Must be busy' claims a level of certainty that isn't justified. You'd use 'must' if you had clear evidence — not when you're guessing.
'She will be busy' states it as a fact. That's too confident when you genuinely have no idea why she hasn't replied.

sentences.context: Your colleague left the office early yesterday without saying anything. You don't know why.

modals.note: You're imagining possibilities without any real evidence. 'Might have' is honest about that uncertainty.
Similar to 'might have'. 'Could have' sometimes carries a slightly more exploratory feel — one option among others.
The formal equivalent of 'might have'. Common in written English and professional speech.
'Must have had an appointment' implies you're reasoning from evidence. But the context says you have no idea — so 'must have' overclaims your certainty.
'Should have had an appointment' implies he was supposed to but perhaps didn't. It's a completely different function — criticism or unfulfilled expectation.

sentences.context: Imagining a hypothetical situation — you don't actually have a million pounds.
sentences.before: If I had a million pounds, I

modals.note: The classic second conditional main clause. 'Would' presents the outcome as a firm intention — what you'd definitely do.
'Could' shifts the meaning slightly — it's no longer about intention but about what would become possible. 'I'd have the means to travel.'
'Might' suggests you're not even sure what you'd do. More honest than 'would' if you genuinely haven't decided.
'I should travel the world' reads as obligation or advice — not a hypothetical outcome. It doesn't function as a second conditional consequent here.

modals.gloss: Wrong tense — makes it first conditional
modals.note: 'If I had a million pounds, I will travel' mixes tenses incorrectly. 'Will' belongs in the first conditional where the situation is real and possible.

sentences.context: Giving advice to a friend who is unhappy in their job. You are imagining yourself in their position.

modals.note: 'If I were you, I would...' is the classic advice structure in English. Warm and direct without being bossy.
Gentler than 'would' — leaves the decision entirely with your friend. Very polite.
'Could' frames job-hunting as one possibility among others — less directive than 'would'.
Stronger than 'would' — closer to direct advice than a hypothetical. 'If I were you, I should' is slightly old-fashioned but still used.
'If I were you, I must look for a new job' sounds unnatural — 'must' doesn't fit comfortably in second conditional main clauses.

sentences.context: You missed the bus and arrived late for an important meeting. Looking back now.
sentences.before: If I had left ten minutes earlier, I

modals.note: The classic third conditional. You're certain that leaving earlier would have changed the outcome. No doubt about the connection.
'Could have caught' means it would have been possible — but perhaps not guaranteed. Maybe the bus was already pulling away.
The weakest of the three. 'Might have' leaves real doubt — even leaving earlier might not have been enough.
'Should have caught the bus' is a criticism of yourself — implying you were supposed to catch it. It doesn't function as a conditional consequent.
'Must have caught the bus' would be a deduction about what actually happened — not a hypothetical about what would have happened.

sentences.context: A colleague didn't read the project brief carefully. The presentation went badly. Reflecting afterwards.
sentences.before: If she had read the brief properly, the presentation

modals.note: You're certain the outcome would have been different. The link between cause and effect is clear.
'Could have gone better' — the potential was there. Slightly more cautious than 'would have'.

modals.gloss: A real possibility, but not certain
modals.note: Leaves room for doubt — maybe other factors would still have caused problems.
'The presentation should have gone better' is a separate statement of expectation or regret, not a conditional consequence. It doesn't complete the if-clause.

modals.gloss: Deduction about the past — wrong function
modals.note: 'Must have gone better' would be a deduction about what actually happened. Here we're talking about a hypothetical — what would have happened under different circumstances.

sentences.context: A doctor is speaking to a patient with high blood pressure.

modals.gloss: Strong internal obligation — the speaker's authority
modals.note: 'Must' here comes from the doctor's professional judgement. It's a personal instruction — strong and direct.

modals.gloss: External obligation — the medical facts require it
modals.note: 'Have to' shifts the source of obligation outward — it's not just the doctor's opinion, it's what the medical evidence demands.
Very close to 'have to' in meaning. 'Need to' often feels slightly warmer and less authoritative than 'must'.
'Should' is advice, not a requirement. For a doctor dealing with high blood pressure, 'should' may understate the urgency.
'Needn't reduce your salt intake' tells the patient it isn't necessary — the exact opposite of the intended advice.

sentences.context: A colleague asks if they need to submit a timesheet this week — the system is being updated.
sentences.after: submit one this week — the system is down.

modals.note: The system being down removes the requirement. 'Don't have to' correctly signals that submission is optional this week.
Means the same as 'don't have to' but sounds more formal or old-fashioned. Still perfectly correct.
This is the classic learner error. 'Mustn't submit' means it's forbidden — as if submitting would cause a problem. 'Don't have to' means it simply isn't required. Very different.
'Shouldn't submit' implies it would be a bad idea to do so. 'Don't have to' simply removes the obligation without discouraging submission.

modals.gloss: Inability — implies the system blocks submission
modals.note: 'Can't submit' suggests the system prevents it. That may be true, but the intended message is about obligation, not ability.
